title: CyberTCG
description: A full-stack app to build and share decks with AI.
content:
Overview

CyberTCG is a full-stack web application developed as a team project for college. The goal of the project was to build an AI-powered application capable of generating Pokémon card decks based on user prompts. While the long-term vision included AI-generated decks, the initial focus was on building a functional system for manually searching cards and creating decks.

I am particularly proud of this project because it required adapting to multiple new technologies under real development constraints, including Next.js, TypeScript, and API integration.

Technologies Used

  • Next.js
  • TypeScript
  • Tailwind CSS
  • TCGDex API
  • REST API development
  • Database integration (likes, comments, decks)

Development Process

The first major challenge was integrating the TCGDex API into a Next.js + TypeScript environment. I had no prior experience with either Next.js or TypeScript, but I was able to quickly learn how to fetch and handle JSON data from external APIs.

Once API data retrieval was working, I implemented the frontend card display system using Tailwind CSS. After several iterations, I successfully built a responsive grid layout that displayed card results dynamically based on user searches.

Features

  • Card search using external TCGDex API
  • Dynamic grid display of Pokémon cards
  • User authentication and deck creation system
  • Public and private decks
  • Like and comment functionality on public decks

Backend & Collaboration

After initial frontend functionality was completed, I worked on extending the backend system to support social features. This included implementing API endpoints for likes and comments, which updated the database in real time.

One important design decision was correcting how comments were stored. Initially, usernames were used as identifiers, but after reviewing the system with the team, we identified that usernames are not unique and could cause conflicts. This was corrected by updating the system to use unique user identifiers instead.

Teamwork & Challenges

This project involved a team of four developers. Compared to previous group projects, task distribution was much more balanced, and each member contributed meaningfully to the system.

Communication challenges still existed at times, particularly around task ownership and progress tracking, but overall the collaboration was effective and resulted in a functional product that met most of the original goals.

What I Learned

  • Working with Next.js and TypeScript in a real project
  • Integrating third-party APIs (TCGDex)
  • Designing RESTful API endpoints for social features
  • Database-driven features like likes and comments
  • Collaborating effectively in a full-stack team environment

Conclusion

This project demonstrates my ability to adapt quickly to new technologies and contribute meaningfully to a full-stack team environment. Despite starting with no prior experience in Next.js or TypeScript, I was able to build core features including API integration, UI rendering, and backend functionality. It also highlights my ability to identify and correct design flaws in a collaborative codebase.

title: AWS Cloud Project
content:
Overview

This project was a case study completed during a Cloud Computing course in the Computer Programming & Analysis program. I am particularly proud of this project because our group accomplished significantly more than originally planned, and it gave me hands-on experience with several AWS services that I had little prior knowledge of.

At the start of the project, I had very limited understanding of AWS Lambda. By the end, I successfully developed multiple Lambda functions to support a static web application, including user authentication, password reset functionality, user registration, and database interactions.

Technologies Used

  • AWS Lambda
  • Amazon API Gateway
  • Static Web Hosting
  • Database integration (for users and tickets)

Development Process

The application used a static frontend that communicated with backend Lambda functions through API Gateway. I gained valuable experience designing and connecting APIs to handle requests such as login, registration, password resets, and data retrieval.

While the technical implementation was successful, the group workflow could have been improved. Task distribution was not clearly defined, and I often took initiative to ensure progress was made. In hindsight, assigning responsibilities earlier and planning the application’s full scope in advance would have resulted in a more efficient development process.

Features

  • User registration and login system
  • Password reset functionality
  • Backend API using AWS Lambda and API Gateway
  • Customer support ticket system (basic implementation)

Limitations & Improvements

Some features were limited due to the constraints of the AWS student sandbox environment. For example, password reset functionality could not send emails because access to AWS messaging services was restricted.

Additionally, the ticket retrieval system could be improved. Currently, it fetches all tickets from the database without pagination or organization, which would not scale well in a real-world application.

Future improvements would include implementing proper pagination, improving data structure, and integrating a full notification system for password resets and ticket responses.

What I Learned

  • How to design and implement AWS Lambda functions
  • Using Amazon API Gateway to connect frontend and backend services
  • Building serverless applications
  • Adapting quickly and learning new technologies independently

Conclusion

This project demonstrates my ability to learn and apply new technologies in a short amount of time. Despite starting with limited knowledge of AWS Lambda, I was able to build fully functional backend services for a web application. It also highlights my initiative and problem-solving skills when working in a team environment.

Note: This project was developed in a temporary AWS student environment, so a live version is not available.

title: ValheimBackupTool
description: A save and backup manager for Valheim.
content:
Overview

A personal project that I’m proud of from the Computer Programming & Analysis course is a backup manager I built for the game Valheim. At the time, there was no easy way to copy save files unless you manually navigated to the saves folder. Since I was frequently backing up my world and character saves, I decided to create an application to simplify the process. To my knowledge, there was no publicly available tool for this when I created it.

Development Process

The project progressed smoothly overall. I started by building the world backup manager. One of the main challenges was designing a file naming system. Initially, I considered using random characters to ensure unique filenames, but this made the backups difficult to organize.

I instead implemented a naming system based on date and time, allowing backups to be displayed in a clean, chronological order from newest to oldest. Once the world backup system was working, implementing character backups was straightforward since the logic was very similar.

One challenge was reusing a single form for both world and character backup management. Depending on the previous user action, the form dynamically adjusted its behavior, allowing users to load or delete backups without duplicating UI components.

Features

  • Automatic backup creation for world and character saves
  • Date and time-based file naming for easy organization
  • Shared management interface for both worlds and characters
  • Automatic detection of the game save folder on first launch
  • Manual folder selection if automatic detection fails

Improvements & Limitations

After getting the application working for my own setup, I updated it to support other Windows systems by removing hardcoded file paths. The app now detects the save folder automatically or prompts the user to select it.

However, there are still areas for improvement. For example, the app does not currently validate whether the selected folder is correct, and there is no way to manually change the save directory after the first launch. These are features I would add in a future update.

What I Learned

  • File management in C# (copying, deleting, and organizing files)
  • Using Environment.GetFolderPath to access system directories
  • Designing reusable UI components in Windows Forms

Conclusion

This project demonstrates my ability to identify a real-world problem and develop a practical solution. It highlights my skills in C# and Windows Forms development, as well as my ability to design functional and user-friendly applications. The program works as intended and has no known bugs.

title: SiegeXY
description: A multiplayer game using NodeJS and Colyseus.
content:
Overview

SiegeXY is a multiplayer browser game developed as a collaborative high school project. The initial goal was to create a simple 2D game where a player could move around and eliminate enemies. Over time, the project evolved into a real-time multiplayer game where multiple players could connect, interact, and compete against each other in the same game world.

This was my first experience working with Node.js, and by the end of the project, I had built a fully functional multiplayer game hosted on a web server.

Technologies Used

  • Node.js
  • WebSockets / Real-time communication
  • Colyseus (multiplayer framework)
  • HTML5 Canvas (game rendering)

Development Process

At the start of the project, I took on the role of main programmer while my partner focused on visual design and art. We began by implementing basic player movement, but quickly realized that a fixed camera system would not scale well for a larger game world.

To solve this, we redesigned the system so that the player remains centered on the screen while the world moves around them, similar to games like Pokémon. This significantly improved gameplay flexibility and map design.

One of the more challenging features was implementing player rotation so that characters always face the cursor. I learned the required vector math by reviewing another student's similar implementation of projectile aiming, which I adapted successfully for this project.

Features

  • Real-time multiplayer gameplay
  • Player movement with centered camera system
  • Mouse-based aiming and shooting mechanics
  • Enemy AI that tracks and attacks players
  • Character selection system
  • Free-for-all multiplayer gameplay mode

Challenges & Improvements

One challenge was the lack of initial planning regarding the full scope of the game. Because we had no prior Node.js experience, we started with very simple goals and expanded the project incrementally as we learned.

If given more time, we would have implemented structured game modes such as capture the flag or objective-based gameplay instead of only free-for-all matches.

What I Learned

  • Building real-time multiplayer applications using Node.js
  • Using Colyseus to manage game state synchronization
  • Implementing vector math for aiming and rotation
  • Designing scalable game architecture for multiplayer systems
  • Learning new technologies independently under project constraints

Conclusion

This project demonstrates my ability to quickly learn and apply new technologies in a real-world scenario. Despite having no prior Node.js experience, I was able to develop a fully functional multiplayer game with real-time interaction features. It also highlights my problem-solving skills and ability to adapt system design as project requirements evolve.
